name: Spectacles Choice Framework
description: A structured visual-analysis framework for evaluating spectacle choices based on facial aesthetics about observable evidence and non-diagnostic language.

principles.principle: A single-color or tonal outfit spanning head to toe with minimal interruption creates an unbroken vertical visual path that may elongate the perceived silhouette.
A strong tonal or structural contrast at the waist zone visually divides the figure into upper and lower segments.
The apparent width of the shoulder line relative to the hip line determines whether the silhouette reads as balanced, top-heavy, or bottom-heavy.
Vertical seams, plackets, stripes, openings, or garment edges may increase perceived vertical length.
Horizontal lines and color blocks may increase perceived width in the zone where they appear.
Garments aligned closely to the body create clearer silhouette edges than garments with uncontrolled excess fabric.
The visual ratio between upper and lower garment zones influences perceived body proportion.
Garment hemlines terminating near visual thirds interact differently with body proportion than midpoint hemlines.
Oversized garments redistribute silhouette volume outward from the body and alter perceived mass distribution.
Layering introduces additional edges and boundaries that either preserve or disrupt silhouette continuity.
Matching tonal values between footwear and lower garments reduces interruption at the hemline.
Neckline geometry frames the face and directs visual attention inward, downward, or horizontally.
Waist definition creates a narrowing at the midsection that structures upper-to-lower proportional reading.
Hem width determines whether the silhouette base reads as narrow, columnar, tapered, or flared.
Visual weight is distributed through color, volume, texture, and structure across garment zones.
Warm or cool garment undertones create harmony or contrast relative to visible skin undertones.
High tonal or chromatic contrast concentrates visual attention at the contrast boundary.
Low tonal contrast across garments creates a unified visual field with reduced segmentation.
Complementary hues create visible chromatic tension and focal boundaries.
Adjacent hues on the color wheel produce low-tension chromatic harmony.
Neutral palettes reduce chromatic complexity and emphasize silhouette and structure.
A single accent color in a restrained palette becomes a dominant focal element.
Overall saturation level influences whether an outfit reads as high-energy or restrained.
High-drape fabrics create soft, gravity-following silhouette edges.
Low-drape fabrics project structured silhouette edges independent of body contour.
Visible tension lines indicate garment stress and possible fit compression in that zone.
Excess visible fabric may indicate looseness or intentional oversized design.
Shoulder seam placement relative to the shoulder crest is a core tailoring fit indicator.
Trouser break amount determines the visual finish and proportional reading of the lower silhouette.
Contrasting textures create visual differentiation without relying on color contrast.
Texture variation within a monochrome palette creates depth while preserving tonal continuity.
Pattern scale differentiation reduces visual conflict when combining multiple prints.
Formalwear is associated with specific observable silhouette and garment markers.
Business formal attire is associated with structured tailoring, conservative palettes, and restrained visual signals.

forbidden_claims: Do not infer attractiveness, desirability, or romantic value.
Do not infer intelligence, competence, wealth, or status.
Do not infer personality traits from clothing.
Do not infer gender identity, sexuality, ethnicity, religion, or politics.
Do not diagnose body shape, health condition, or eating disorder.
Do not make moral judgments about clothing choices.
Do not use absolute language such as 'flattering' or 'unflattering' as objective fact.
Do not frame body proportions as defects or problems.
Do not provide prescriptive beauty rankings.
Do not infer socioeconomic class from styling choices.
Do not state seasonal color analysis results as objective truth.
Do not claim intent unless visually supported by observable evidence.
